We know it's fake, but the fact that it is, we consider it unnecessary to shout it from the rooftops. That was the magic that characterized wrestling, and now it's dissipated. Now they tell you it's a fake show directly to your face. Which I consider unnecessary because Marvel actors and directors don't tell you "come enjoy our movies with fake effects and green screens." Of course not, because the magic has to be preserved to enhance the experience. Now kayfabe is officially dead, thanks to Triple H and TKO.

Yeah I understand and agree with protecting the business, but nowadays you have wrestlers on the podcast circuit talking openly about backstage and production stuff.  Nothing too juicy.  So I imagine this series won't go too deeply into booking politics and locker room drama.  

 I hope the series accomplishes two things.  

1)  Helps casual audiences understand why wrestlers call this "scripted" and not "fake."  

2)  Help challenge the IWC perception that top wrestlers (i.e. "so and so Hogan") have more power over the booking than believed.  It's always competitive because there are so few TV spots available on the shows, and people regularly get let go.

Kayfabe is dead, sure, but I agree with CM Punk that stripping away the veil is risky. Reality content like Tough Enough/LFG is one thing; this is main roster, and there's already a lot of negative social media content being churned around Raw and Smackdown every week.  I hope HHH/Paul knows what they're doing here.

Watching the AJ Styles documentary and everything that he went through until his entrance at the Royal Rumble made me appreciate him so much more.  I think that it helps bring in casual fans like myself where we can get interested in the performers in addition to the storyline that they working through.
